data.body:
=== RESEARCH REPORT ===
=== DSO ticket-data mining for complexity-proportionate tuning
=== agent a47ef2e06ccde24b6

All verified. I have everything I need. Here is the complete report.

---

# DSO Ticket-Data Mining: Tuning a Complexity-Proportionate Plan-Review Gate

## 1. Where & how the data lives

The DSO ticket store is an **event-sourced rebar store** living in two coupled places under `~/digital-service-orchestra`:

- **Orphan `tickets` git branch** — the canonical, shareable event log (commits like `ticket: STATUS …`, `ticket: COMMENT …`, `suggestion: RECORD`, `chore: sync events from DSO reconciler`). Read it without checkout via `git -C ~/digital-service-orchestra show tickets:<path>`.
- **`.tickets-tracker/` worktree** (gitignored; `.git` → `…/.git/worktrees/-tickets-tracker`) — the checked-out form. **One directory per ticket**, named by ticket id (e.g. `0004-e191-dc15-4a4b/`).

Inside each ticket dir:
- **Event files** `<timestamp>-<uuid>-<EVENTTYPE>.json` — the immutable log. Event types in the store: `BRIDGE_ALERT` (74,447 — Jira bridge noise), `ARCHIVED` (7,874), `SNAPSHOT` (7,109 — compaction checkpoints), `SYNC` (2,799), `CREATE` (2,168), `EDIT` (1,719), `STATUS` (1,328), `COMMENT` (888), `LINK` (594), `VERIFY_COMMANDS` (84), `PRECONDITIONS` (35), `UNLINK` (33).
- **`.cache.json`** — the **compiled head state** of the ticket (the easiest bulk-read path). Holds `ticket_type, status, title, description, tags, comments[], deps[], file_impact[], parent_id, priority, verify_commands, signature, reverts`, etc.

**How to read (read-only):** I parsed all `.cache.json` files directly in Python — no mutation. The installed `rebar` CLI (`~/.local/bin/rebar`, an older/DSO variant) also reads this store (`rebar list --type=… --status=… --output llm`), but bulk distribution work is faster against the cache files. Reading via the orphan branch (`git show tickets:<id>/.cache.json`) is the zero-touch alternative.

There are **23,222 ticket directories** but only **10,872 carry a compiled `.cache.json`** (the rest are bridge/suggestion/tombstone artifacts). The 10,872 split into **8,317 native DSO tickets** and **2,555 Jira-imported** (`jira-…` ids, almost all empty-description `imported:reconciler-bootstrap` stubs — excluded from all analysis below as noise).

## 2. Corpus characterization (native: 8,317 tickets)

**Type breakdown:** task 4,455 · bug 2,112 · story 1,362 · epic 388.
**Status totals:** closed 6,842 · deleted 791 · open 583 · archived 92 · in_progress 9. (So ~82% closed — a mostly-resolved corpus.)

**Distributions by type** (med / p75 / p90 / max):

| Type | desc_len (chars) | AC count | file_impact | links (deps) | children | comments | has `## Acceptance Criteria` |
|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|------|
| task (4,455) | 1167 / 2267 / 3330 / 10392 | 0 / 4 / 7 / 15 | ~0 | 0 / 1 / 1 / 8 | 0 / 0 / 0 / 4 | 0 / 2 / 7 / 22 | **50%** |
| bug (2,112) | 825 / 1880 / 3283 / 11638 | 0 / 0 / 0 / 5 | 0 | 0 / 0 / 0 / 3 | 0 | 1 / 2 / 4 / 30 | **1%** |
| story (1,362) | 2021 / 2777 / 3723 / 13743 | 0 / 0 / 0 / 10 | 0 | 1 / 2 / 4 / 12 | 2 / 3 / 5 / 18 | 0% |
| epic (388) | 2903 / 6361 / 11996 / 52171 | 0 / 0 / 0 / 6 | 0 | 0 / 2 / 4 / 23 | 3 / 7 / 10 / 26 | 0% |

**Key structural facts that shape the gate design:**
- **`file_impact` is effectively unused** — exactly **1 ticket** in the entire store has any entry, and that entry is a placeholder (`{"path":"The ticket description/requirements","reason":"modified"}`). **You cannot tune a gate on stored file_impact in this corpus.**
- **AC checklists only exist on tasks** (50% of tasks have an `## Acceptance Criteria` block; bugs 1%, stories/epics ~0%). Bugs/stories/epics encode "size" differently — bugs in their reproduction/fix rationale, stories/epics in **children count**.
- **`verify_commands`**: present on only 81 stories; `signature` (completion attestation): **0 tickets** — the completion-verification close gate was not signing in this store.
- **Stored complexity classification is sparse.** Only **2 tickets carry a `COMPLEXITY_CLASSIFICATION:COMPLEX` tag** (`b591-d4ec`, `7f2a-8fa4`, both closed stories). The complexity-evaluator's verdict is otherwise captured **ad hoc in comments**: scanning comment bodies yields **59 tickets** with an explicit `COMPLEXITY: <class>` marker — **TRIVIAL 11, SIMPLE 3, MODERATE 19, COMPLEX 26**; by type **48 bugs, 7 epics, 4 stories**. This is the de-facto labeled set (small, bug-skewed).

**DSO's own rubric** (from `plugins/dso/agents/complexity-evaluator.md` + `scripts/blast-radius-score.py`, `scripts/review-complexity-classifier.sh`, `hooks/lib/centrality.sh`) — the thresholds the corpus was generated under:
- 8-dimension classifier → **TRIVIAL/SIMPLE → "minimal"**, **MODERATE → "standard"**, **COMPLEX → "deep"** review depth.
- File bands: **≤1 → TRIVIAL/SIMPLE · 2–3 → MODERATE · >3 → COMPLEX**. Layer bands: **≤1 / 2 / ≥3**. Any interface change, Low scope-certainty, or `blast_radius complex_override` force COMPLEX. Epics: **>6 success criteria → COMPLEX**.
- `blast-radius-score.py`: per-file depth+pattern+dir-boost score; **score > 5 → `complex_override=true`** (forces COMPLEX); emits `layer_count` (distinct top-level dirs). **Not persisted to tickets** — printed/consumed inline.
- `review-complexity-classifier.sh` (diff-based): 7 factors summing to ≤17 → **`computed_total ≥7 → "deep"`, `≥3 → "standard"`, `<3 → "light"`**; size overlay **upgrade at 300 added lines, warn at 600**; "safeguard/critical-path/anti-shortcut" files floor the score at 3.
- `centrality.sh`: counts import/source fan-in for a file (integer, 0 on none); no fixed band — used as an evidence modifier. **Not persisted.**

## 3. Stratified example library (real IDs, with signals)

Drawn from the 59 explicitly-labeled tickets (label = complexity-evaluator's own verdict in-comment). AC/file_impact are ~0 for bugs by construction; the discriminating signal is the file/subsystem count in the rationale (shown where extracted).

**TRIVIAL**
- `2ac9-fabe-bbb9-4af2` (bug/closed) — desc 3318c, 0 links, 2 cmts — "long batch dispatches in closure-checks classifier"; rationale `COMPLEXITY: TRIVIAL | FIXABLE: Yes`.
- `6da6-410d-e2d7-49a9` (bug/closed) — desc 849c, 5 cmts — `t_restage_noop_*` test.
- `001c-f1ed` (bug/closed) — desc 1449c — "3 concurrent lock tickets created."
- `f63c-1ca6-b210-4fa5` (story/closed) — desc 3731c, 2 links.

**SIMPLE**
- `9d55-e6c3` (bug/closed) — desc 397c — rationale `Complexity=simple/single-file (0)`.
- `3ffe-d103` (bug/closed) — desc 1011c — relative-path bug.
- `41da-6b3b` (bug/closed) — desc 220c, 6 cmts.

**MODERATE** (rationale cites 2–5 files / 2 subsystems)
- `19a3-03ca-6c89-4e1b` (bug/closed) — desc 3509c, 2 links, 11 cmts — `resolve_ticket_id` scan over ~20K dirs; rationale `MODERATE — 1-3 files in single subsystem`.
- `b6e3-e771-d37b-436c` (bug/closed) — desc 2785c — version-bump pipeline; rationale `MODERATE (1-2 scripts + 3-5 tests, 2 subsystems)`.
- `e710-f43a-494a-4d86` (bug/closed) — rationale `MODERATE (two instruction-file edits + one script + two test additions)`.
- `9894-a463-090a-43e5` (bug/closed) — desc 3431c, 6 cmts.

**COMPLEX** (rationale cites 6–117 files / 3–5 subsystems)
- `b575-ac1c-f720-4839` (epic/closed) — **desc 31,006c, 6 links, 23 children, 17 cmts** — "Cycle-end arbiter + workflow unification"; rationale `COMPLEX (scope_certainty: High)`.
- `fe66-f05b-0687-46b9` (bug/closed) — rationale `COMPLEX — 6 files / 4 subsystems … escalating to epic`.
- `3c53-5ae3-6825-4c0b` (bug/closed) — desc 7346c.
- `ee3a-43fc-652b-49ff` (bug/closed) — desc 3436c — "2 hallucinated findings."

**Epics-with-children** (the structural-complexity exemplars; children counted across the store):
- `5ee9-143e` (archived) — **26 children**, 4 links, desc 4348c — merge-state-detection redesign.
- `4e55-a6ba` (closed) — 25 children, desc 10742c.
- `3a03-b3f2-b34c-4e4f` (closed) — 24 children, desc 13032c — level-triggered reconciler.
- `170b-2ac9` (closed) — 24 children — commit-level source attribution.
- `b575-ac1c-f720-4839` (closed) — 23 children (also COMPLEX-labeled).

## 4. Proposed thresholds (grounded in the distributions)

**(a) Complexity → review-depth tiers.** Map to DSO's own minimal/standard/deep, but driven by the signals that are actually populated in this corpus (children for stories/epics; file/subsystem count for tasks/bugs — read from the rationale or recompute from the diff, **not** from stored file_impact, which is empty):

| Tier | Tasks/Bugs (files · layers) | Stories/Epics (children · links) | Backed by |
|------|------|------|------|
| **minimal** | ≤1 file, 1 layer | 0–1 children, ≤1 link | TRIVIAL set: file-counts {1,2}, no multi-subsystem; story/epic children med 0–2 |
| **standard** | 2–3 files, ≤2 layers / 2 subsystems | 2–6 children (story p75=3, epic p75=7), 2–4 links | MODERATE set: file-counts {2,3,5}, subsystem={2,2}; story children p75=3 |
| **deep** | ≥4 files OR ≥3 layers/subsystems OR any interface change | **≥7 children** OR ≥5 links OR desc ≥ ~12k chars | COMPLEX set: file-counts {6,11,117}, subsystems {3,3,3,3,3,4,4,5}; epic kids p90=10 |

The **file 2–3 / >3** and **subsystem 3** cutoffs are not invented — they fall exactly on the boundaries DSO's evaluator used and that the labeled tickets' rationale text confirms (TRIVIAL ≤2 files, MODERATE 2–5/2-subsystem, COMPLEX 6+/3+-subsystem). For containers, the **children=7** cutoff sits at epic p75 and is where rework risk jumps sharply (see §5).

**(b) Escalation-to-a-stronger-model factor = SIZE × COMPLEXITY × CENTRALITY.** Compute a weighted score and escalate the planner/reviewer model when it crosses a band:

- **SIZE**: diff lines (DSO's own `upgrade ≥300`, `warn ≥600`), or desc length (epic p90 ≈ 12k chars as a "large container" flag).
- **COMPLEXITY**: the tier from (a) — minimal 0 / standard 1 / deep 2.
- **CENTRALITY/BLAST-RADIUS**: reuse DSO's existing computations. `blast-radius-score.py` already emits a `score` with a **>5 → force-COMPLEX** convention and a `layer_count`; `centrality.sh` emits import fan-in. Neither is persisted today, so the gate should **call them at plan time and persist the result onto the ticket** (a tag like `centrality:N` / `blast:N`, or a structured field) — that single change makes future tuning data-driven instead of comment-scraping.
- **Escalation rule (proposed):** escalate to the stronger model when **tier = deep AND (blast-radius score > 5 OR centrality fan-in ≥ 5 OR diff ≥ 300 lines)**. The `>5` and the floor-at-3 safeguard rules are DSO's own; the fan-in `≥5` mirrors the `blast_radius` factor's "≥5 refs → 2" band in `review-complexity-classifier.sh`.

**(c) Default chunking granularity.** Batch **3–5 acceptance criteria per LLM call**. Justification from the distribution: AC counts where they exist (tasks) sit at **p75=4, p90=7, max=15**; epic success-criteria overflow flips to COMPLEX at **>6** in DSO's rubric. So 3–5 keeps the common case (≤p75) in a single call while splitting the long tail (p90+) into 2 batches — and never lets a single call exceed the 6-criterion threshold the rubric itself treats as "too big to be simple."

## 5. Honest assessment of the corpus as a tuning/eval set

**What it's genuinely good for:**
- **The 59 in-comment complexity labels are a usable weak-gold seed.** They are the evaluator's *own* verdicts with rationale text (file/subsystem counts), so they let you regression-test a re-implemented classifier against DSO's prior behavior. Their internal consistency checks out: extracted file counts rise cleanly across labels (TRIVIAL 1–2 → MODERATE 2–5 → COMPLEX 6–117), and subsystem counts (MODERATE=2, COMPLEX 3–5) match the rubric's bands.
- **Rework signals correlate with complexity — a real over/under-review proxy.** A regression/rework marker (regex over description+comments for `regression | didn't actually fix | still failing | second attempt | reopen`) appears in **TRIVIAL 18% → SIMPLE 33% → MODERATE 32% → COMPLEX 38%** of labeled tickets — monotonic with complexity. The strongest single relationship: **epics with ≥7 children carry rework markers 42% of the time vs 12% for epics with <7 children (46/110 vs 34/278)** — a 3.5× gradient that directly validates children-count as an escalation/deep-review trigger. Corpus-wide rework keyword volume: `regression` 551 tickets, `superseded` 129, `reopen` 43, `not-actually-fixed` 24; `supersedes` links 24, plus 7 `duplicates`.

**Limitations — be skeptical:**
- **No clean reopen/supersede outcome field.** `reverts` is empty on every ticket; STATUS-event reopens are nearly invisible (only **4 native tickets** show a closed→open transition) because compaction collapses history into SNAPSHOTs and discards intermediate STATUS events. So "rework" must be inferred from **text markers**, which is noisy and recall-limited — treat it as a soft label, not ground truth.
- **Small, bug-skewed labeled set.** 59 labels, 48 of them bugs, only 4 stories and 7 epics. Tier (a)'s task/bug file-count cutoffs rest on ~30 data points; the story/epic side leans on structural children-distributions, not labels.
- **`file_impact` is dead in this corpus** — you must derive size from diffs/rationale, not the stored field. Any gate that reads `file_impact` will see zeros.
- **Selection bias / DSO-specificity.** This is one self-hosted, agent-generated repo: bug-heavy (2,112 bugs), shell/CLI-centric, with DSO-idiomatic "subsystems" (scripts/, hooks/, skills/, CLAUDE.md policy). The file/subsystem thresholds and the "safeguard-file floors" are tuned to DSO's layout and **will not transfer cleanly to a diverse client base** (different layer conventions, different blast-radius topology, real product code rather than tooling). Use it to calibrate the *shape* of the gate and as a regression fixture; re-derive the numeric bands per client.

**Bottom line:** the corpus is a defensible *tuning + regression* set (validate the classifier reproduces the 59 prior verdicts; confirm the children≥7 and file>3 cutoffs track rework rate) but **not** a clean held-out eval set — labels are sparse and bug-biased, outcomes are text-inferred, and the topology is DSO-specific. The single highest-leverage fix for future tuning is to **persist the already-computed blast-radius and centrality scores onto each ticket** at plan time, converting today's comment-scraping into a real labeled dataset.

*(Analysis was strictly read-only; no DSO ticket files, branches, or events were modified.)*
